©2022-2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Muscle Testing Basics There are two types of muscle testing used in QAT work. 1. We can use moderate force to test a muscle and determine if it is functioning in a manner that supports the client's movement and posture. This method is taught in 
the QAT home study course. There are videos, pictures, and written materials to 
assist you in learning that method of muscle testing. It is a valuable skillset to have, as it demonstrates real – time results with the QAT 
quantum devices to skeptical clients. The therapist identifies a few muscles that are 
significantly weaker compared to their contralateral counterparts or lose their 
contraction quickly. Then, the therapist optimizes those muscles with the QAT 
process. Test again, and the client will be impressed or even sometimes baffled 
when those muscles have regained their ability to sustain a moderate contraction. This is how to “sell” the work. 2. The other type of muscle testing in QAT is to find a few muscles to use in testing for the presence of stored trauma or a lack of connection to the brain. These muscles 
are called indicator muscles. In this type of muscle testing, we are not actually 
testing muscles but the reflexive action of the nervous system’s response to a 
challenge. It might be more accurately stated that we are employing neurological 
manual muscle testing with this method of muscle testing. The therapist requests the client to lock a muscle into a static position in response to a brief 
and gradual push or pull. This is not a contest of strength between the therapist and the 
client. All we want to know is if the client’s nervous system can recognize a brief challenge 
to a muscle and reflexively lock it into position. Practice with the client until they recognize 
the difference between a wrestling match and muscle testing. Either the muscle locks into 
position and holds, or it cannot do so and allows the muscle to unlock. The terminology commonly used in applied kinesiology is a strong versus weak muscle. That 
can be misleading. It is a matter of whether or not the muscle can neurologically turn on and 
stay on during the challenge. It is better to tell the client that the muscle is locked or 
unlocked when testing instead of strong or weak. 11

©2022-2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Major Points for Indicator Muscle Testing Have the client remove all electronics, magnets, chewing gum, and energetic items. A muscle that unlocks may not literally unlock its contraction but instead will drift, shake 
erratically, or feel spongy during the testing. There are different degrees of failure to lock. Correct anything short of a crisp, sustained lock. A locked muscle responds very quickly and holds its position during the testing. Instruct the client to keep their teeth slightly apart, not hold their breath, or close their eyes 
during the testing. The same applies to the therapist. A test will be invalid if the client moves their body around to recruit other muscles during 
the testing. The therapist should not squeeze the client’s body part during the testing. We want to avoid 
creating discomfort for the client, which may interfere with the testing. The client’s hands do not touch the body during testing, and the feet are uncrossed. The therapist says, Hold. Wait 1-2 seconds. Then, introduce a light, constant force for 1-1.5 
sec, increasing up to 1-2 pounds (0.5-1 kg) of pressure until the client’s muscle locks or not. The muscle should lock within 2 inches (5 cm) of movement and remain locked for 1-1.5 
seconds. The therapist does not make sudden, rapid movements or repeated pumping 
actions. The client must be engaged in the process. The result will be invalid if they space out or are 
distracted by a thought during the testing. With some clients, it is essential to keep them in 
the present moment by maintaining verbal engagement. Repeat the tests several times if 
necessary to ensure the correct response is unlocked. Switch to another indicator muscle if the responses become more erratic during a session. If 
it continues, the person may be dehydrated. Expectations During Testing We want unbiased data. Expectations, whether conscious or unconscious, will impact the 
results. Refrain from anticipating as specific response to a test. 13

©2022-2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Evaluating Indicator Muscles (IM) for Use During a QAT 
Session The simplest way to conduct a QAT session is to use an Indicator Muscle (IM). One or more indicator muscles are used during a session to indicate whether or not issues 
are present. The six muscles we will use are the anterior deltoid, brachioradialis, supraspinatus, 
rhomboids (in both supine and prone positions), quadriceps, and the piriformis in the prone 
position. If the client experiences shoulder or hip pain on either side while using a muscle, 
we may need to identify alternative muscles to use as indicator muscles or consider using a 
surrogate. When testing whether a particular muscle can be used as an IM, we must determine 
whether it can turn on and off. To do that, challenge the muscle by moving the joint to 
cause the muscle to contract. If it locks, we know that it can turn on. Muscle Spindle Cell Test Test a muscle to see if it can lock. We cannot use it as an indicator muscle if it does not lock. Use your thumb and a couple of fingers to push firmly towards each other on the muscle 
belly. Immediately rechallenge the muscle. It should now unlock. A muscle must be able to 
lock and unlock reliably to be used as an indicator muscle. To have the muscle lock again, 
pull apart the muscle belly. This test utilizes the mechano receptors known as spindle cells 
to send messages to the brain. The brain will automatically reset the muscle in about 10 
seconds. Magnet Test - an alternative to the muscle spindle cell test Test a muscle to see if it can lock. If it locks, good. Place a strong neodymium magnet on the 
CV or GV meridian. The muscle should now unlock. If it unlocks, that muscle can be used as 
an indicator muscle. 14

lead to neurological disorganization. Switching was a concept developed by Dr. George 
Goodheart for the modality of Applied Kinesiology in the 1960s. We need to evaluate and 
correct neurological disorganization issues to adequately assess the client’s neurological 
response to the therapeutic challenges introduced by the therapist. Why do we care about neurological disorganization? We need valid answers from the 
client’s nervous system. If we have reliable testing, we can perform muscle testing to 
identify potential deficiencies, imbalances, or suboptimal connections to the brain’s sensory 
– motor cortex, as well as issues with the acupuncture system. Many of us have had experiences where we have witnessed people in health food stores 
and other places where the salesperson grabs an arm and starts muscle testing. There are so 
many things wrong with that type of action. One, the salesperson or practitioner may not be 
receiving a valid answer. The other major problem is that if the operator’s income depends 
on the outcome of the response, then operator bias can taint the results. When performing 
muscle testing, the tester must have no interest in the potential response. By following these procedures, we can identify clients whose nervous systems cannot be 
reasonably organized for at least a short period on that day. If the client has recently 
experienced a strong emotional shock, we may need to obtain a surrogate for testing 
procedures. The surrogate should be first cleared of neurological disorganization before 
using that person as an assistant. 19

personal user. The QAT EMF Harmonizer sticker can be placed in a cell phone case,

©2022-2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Eyes Into Distortion Evaluation (Optional) While this step is optional, it is recommended to conduct this testing for individuals wearing 
prism glasses or those with other eye-tracking disorders. Individuals with neuromuscular 
disorders or vestibular issues would also be good candidates. 1. No QAT devices are used in this procedure. 2. Test IM. It should test locked. 3. We will be asking the client to move their eyes in a total of six directions. The client shall not turn their head, move their jaw laterally, clench their teeth, or 
hold their breath. If their eyes flutter or they cannot maintain the extreme position at the end of the 
range of motion, that would indicate a neurological problem. There is no need to 
test the IM if that occurs. When they have reached the end of the range of motion for the eye in each 
direction by following the therapist's finger, the therapist will activate the IM. 4. IM should test locked if normal. 5. If IM tests unlocked, that would suggest a possible cranial fault, AO or upper cervical restrictions, or a TMJ issue, usually on the same side where the eyes failed. Ideally, every person should be able to hold their eyes in any of these six directions 
while keeping an indicator muscle locked. The directions include: • Lateral right and left • Lower right and left • Upper right and left 24
section: 4. IM should test locked if normal.

©2022-2025 NRT Trainings, LLC What are Portals? From the mystical viewpoint, portals are openings into other realities or dimensions. In 
those esoteric teachings, it is known that one can purposely or accidentally open portals. Meetings with other entities and knowledge of other things can be gained. This contact 
could be a good or a bad thing. For the science of quantum mechanics, physicists can explain these portals or openings, 
which are predicted by String Theory, a theory that describes multiple dimensions. It has 
been postulated that there may be other beings and universes within these dimensions. If 
true, there could be an intersection of those other realities with our own through portals. Some have experienced these beings using our senses to peer into our reality through 
portals. Portals can be created through the use of mind-altering drugs, addictive drugs, and alcohol. Rape and other violent physical and emotionally traumatic events can also create openings 
through our auras, our body’s electromagnetic field. Certain religious or spiritual practices 
may intentionally or unintentionally open portals. Even acute illnesses or surgeries can
